posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked: Jeeeez…what has happened to the pricing on this??? Avg WS price is $2322 now…some places offering at close to 3k! What’s up? Even the 15 is cheaper?

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: I guess collectors have regarded this wine as pretty squarely in the same class as DRC’s GC’s…anywhere from the middle of the hierarchy to as high as 3rd. Price has been getting closer and closer to reflecting this it seems.

posts.name: Larry Archibald
posts.cooked:
What if it were half as much – would you be a buyer? I think it’s fine when you end up with a wine like this, purchased long ago, in your cellar. Maybe you paid what seemed like a lot at the time. But I wouldn’t come close to buying such a wine now. Way too much money for a bottle of wine! Can it possibly be worth even $600? Not sure I’ve had many wines in that category, though I’ve had quite a few that currently would sell for that or much more.

Just can’t see doing it.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: wine-searcher prices have been getting absurd for many wines in the last year or two. does anyone actually pay these prices or is it just merchants using wine-searcher as a device to prop up the value of their wares?

posts.name: Ed Gonzales
posts.cooked: wow, that’s musigny pricing and then some. but…what robert said!

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: Come on, this is annoying but hardly surprising. It happens whenever the demand for a wine among people with wealth levels of, say, mid-eight figures or more exceeds the supply of a wine that is produced in limited quantities. To put it another way, the number of bottles of Roumier Bonnes Mares that people for whom $2,300 is pocket change want to buy is now greater than the number of bottles of Roumier B-M in the market. The rest of us can therefore say bye-bye to Roumier B-M. Life under capitalism.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
I accuse Buzz of being guilty of click bait posting

I was hoping for an actual tasting note. Sigh, just another pricing thread.

All we need now is some time honoured WB thread drift crap on why burgundy is such bad value for money

Cheers Brodie

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: 1500 at auction. WS results mean nothing for high end burgundy/rhone

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
1500 at auction. WS results mean nothing for high end burgundy/rhone

Exactly. Last auction price I have is $1,447 per bottle.

Winesearcher is still a valuable tool, but in the past couple of years- and at an accelerating rate- the actual supply of really high end bottles in the secondary market has become scarily low. This means far fewer sales listings, and a very high concentration of bottles that are priced for the consumer who wants that particular wine right now and is willing to pay just about any price for it.

Auction data has always been a better barometer of FMV that WS, but the discrepancy has really grown of late the higher up the quality scale you go. And even auction data is getting more volatile since a given wine does not necessarily come to market very often, and you have a very small number of customers bidding- or not bidding- at these prices. Customers who are less price sensitive as well.

Back to winesearcher, in my experience these are very real asking prices. And with the trends of the past decade they eventually become reasonable prices. Meantime, it is all a question of cash flow. If you have a small brick and mortar, it is often not viable to put huge prices on $100K of inventory (which could be 12-24 bottles these days) and wait for years. But if you are one of the major national retailers and also have a huge business in beer and liquor, it is quite easy to have that mega-expensive inventory. It can also be a smart move because when that rare client comes along, you have what they want ready to go- and that is more important to such a client than the price they actually pay.

BTW- don’t be afraid to send an email and make an offer. I picked up some 2012 Roumier not long ago for a very nice price (for today- 5 years ago it would have been considered highway robbery) because I happened to ask the store manager on a day when, for whatever reason, he decided he would give a big discount, still make some good money relative to his original cost, and get some inventory out the door. For best results, make offers right after major holidays or a couple of weeks before annual inventory (when taxes are assessed on existing inventory.)

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: Good advice! Thanks!

posts.name: Maxwell A.
posts.cooked:
Honestly, what’s the most any of you would pay for a bottle of Roumier Bonnes-Mares?

Also/alternatively, what do you think a bottle of Roumier Bonnes-Mares is worth in terms of the quality you’re getting?

I ask because I’ve never tasted Roumier Bonnes-Mares and I’m curious to hear what you all think.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
Honestly, what’s the most any of you would pay for a bottle of Roumier Bonnes-Mares?

Also/alternatively, what do you think a bottle of Roumier Bonnes-Mares is worth in terms of the quality you’re getting?

I ask because I’ve never tasted Roumier Bonnes-Mares and I’m curious to hear what you all think.

I have some older vintages in my cellar, but have only tasted the 93 and the 96 that I can recall, and both were pretty damn good. Of course, every one I have was purchased upon release and well before the absurd escalation in prices, my Roumier BM purchases ending in 2001. Hmmm, just looked and it appears that I bought one bottle of 2010, so I probably stretched a bit for that one, but undoubtedly found it at regular retail pricing through regular distribution and not jacked up gouge-the-consumer what-the-market-will-bear pricing.

As to what a bottle is worth to anyone or what anyone of us would pay for the quality one is getting, it would depend so much on how wealthy someone is and how much they are willing to spend on a bottle. By the end of my active Burgundy buying period, I was chasing bottles in the 2010 vintage that I really wanted and might pay up the maybe $500 for that rare single bottle here and there, as in perhaps this 2010 Roumier BM or a Rousseau Chambertin or a Mugnier Musigny…but only if I could find it at regular mark up through a trusted retailer and not, as I said, gouging prices. Then I came to my senses. I have had some great wines, but I am not sure if I have had more than a very few experiences in 30 years where I thought it was worth $500 to me. And never one that was worth $1000 to me. For some crazy reason I might still part with $500 for a Rousseau Chambertin through proper channels, but $1500-2000? Not a chance.

But if I was worth hundreds of millions or more, and $1000 felt more like $10 to most people, why not? And I suppose that is why prices are where they are right now. But to really stretch yourself financially thinking that you are going to taste the wine and see God? More likely than not your lofty expectations will not be met, unless you convince yourself that they were. And you had better hope you don’t get a corked bottle.

When you read notes on these boards of lavish tastings, I would imagine that in some cases the person has an obscene amount of money, but more commonly they or their friends bought these wines before the prices went haywire.

posts.name: Dave Nerland
posts.cooked: Pricing is crazy. The only bottles I have ever purchased of Roumier Bonnes Mares were the 2005 vintage. I paid about $90 per bottle. Sadly, only 3 bottles available. Took them all.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Unfortunately this is the market we are dealing with currently but I do think there must a correction coming. It seems fairly obvious that speculator activity is a major part of this run up of prices, as their involvement almost always explains the huge premium of younger wines over their older counterparts. And they are getting pretty good returns.

Another clue is The type of wine being bought where you see these price increases. Almost all high end and all in short supply, which is what you want if you invest in wine.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked: And a correction, if severe enough, might drive down those $1500 bottles to $1000-1200, but I think we would have to be in a pretty bad recession or worse to see the market prices go down to a “normal” retail mark up over the normal distribution wholesale price…which might make wines like the Rousseau Chambertin or Roumier Bonnes Mares go down into the $500-600 range, at least in three-tier states like WA.
The wholesale for the 15’ was $500

posts.name: paul rosenberg
posts.cooked:
Pricing is crazy. The only bottles I have ever purchased of Roumier Bonnes Mares were the 2005 vintage. I paid about $90 per bottle. Sadly, only 3 bottles available. Took them all.

i guess i got taken as i paid $275 for the 2005.

i tasted the 2002 recently. great juice, still a baby, very dense while still being light on its feet. for me it easily outclassed a mini-vertical of roty charmes as well as a 2001 mugnier moose tasted on the same evening.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: I believe the wholesale on the 05 was around $200 per bottle. So it seems Dave got a misplaced deal. Unless he’s buying cellar door ?

posts.cooked:

More likely than not your lofty expectations will not be met, unless you convince yourself that they were. And you had better hope you don’t get a corked bottle.

When you read notes on these boards of lavish tastings, I would imagine that in some cases the person has an obscene amount of money, but more commonly they or their friends bought these wines before the prices went haywire.

Yes, Dave got a great deal and good fortune to him. The 2005 retail in my neck of the woods was upper $200s if memory serves.

As for the broader question- I am generally with Robert on this, who I have quoted above.

When I first got into wine, and for a few years thereafter, $500 was the magic number. That was the price of Petrus (in a top vintage), Romanee-Conti and Montrachet, and for all practical purposes the top price for just about any reasonably commercially available wine at release.

In spite of the realities that I have researched and followed over the years and my full understanding of how and why that number has changed, it is hard for me to get over the psychology of that number- especially when it is broken through by wines that were $100 or less back when Romanee-Conti was $500.

And so I think $500 is probably my magic internal number at which I would buy Roumier Bonnes-Mares again.

Obviously that will not happen anytime soon, but considering I picked up a pristine 6 pack of 2010 Drouhin Bonnes-Mares at auction two weeks ago for less than $300 per bottle all-in, I am frankly okay waiting on Roumier and perhaps indefinitely. Drouhin makes an incredible Bonnes-Mares, probably second only to Roumier- and even then that small difference could be as much about style as anything else. And the 2010 is a real thriller. When I can get a case of it for less than $300 a bottle- Roumier at $1,500 a bottle is not even on my radar. That said, if my net income were 7 figures, they would both be on my radar.

Something else the wine drinker has to remember is that one-offs make very bad personal investments for future drinking. One bottle of Roumier Bonnes-Mares is potential profit for an investor- one intact unit is all you need. But if you plan to drink Roumier Bonnes-Mares, or especially if you want to get into Musigny and Amoureuses, you MUST buy at least 3 bottles each vintage, preferably 4-6, and expect that some will be opened too soon, some too late, and that some vintages will surprise to the upside and some to the downside. The $500 experience is not guaranteed with every bottle- and so for wine drinkers it is all the more difficult to even consider paying current prices for certain top wines unless the dollar amount involved is not financially significant to one’s personal wealth.

Sorry if that drifted a bit- but I think the bottom line is that we who drink the wine are looking at a different set of metrics than the investors, and as such purchases of blue chip Burgundies are driven primarily by what we can “afford” within the realm of what that really means for drinking wines and hoping for those rare moments of perfection. And in the case of Roumier Bonnes-Mares, that means 4-6 bottles at release- not just 1.

Mark- to your point, I do think a correction will come, but what worries me is distribution. Let’s say you have a 30-40% market correction in Roumier prices. That removes the flip for the most part- but it still leaves you with long waiting lists for the wines at every point where they are currently sold. With the flip gone, some of those buyers go away- but there would still be many buyers left, many of them eager to have more bottles than they get now. That is what I observed in Texas with Ramonet. For a period of time, the flip potential was lost- but that had little impact on the list of buyers for the wines, and many remained on the waiting list even during that point after premox got serious attention and before Ramonet joined the recent surge in auction pricing along with other top Domaines.

So even with that kind of correction, I fear that I will never again see the days when I could get 3-4 Musigny and Amoureuses, and 6-12 Bonnes-Mares a year. Texas does not even get Roumier anymore- 2013 was the last vintage to my knowledge. And we used to be a very strong market for the wines- one of the largest in the United States.

It is not just a question of overseas demand, but of US distributors in some cases taking the easy road and selling all the wines in fewer spots rather than try to share them around as Diageo used to do.

posts.name: Thomas De Waen
posts.cooked: The days of 3-4 musignys per year were an anomaly. That’s because 1pc of the entire production of one of the most desirable wines in burgundy going to a single consumer!
